abstract: Since coming to power in 2010, the Hungarian government, led by the populist radical right (PRR) Fidesz party, has gained international notoriety for reshaping domestic institutions to fit an illiberal political vision. This systemic democratic backsliding has triggered a protracted conflict with the European Union, reaching a flashpoint in April 2022 when the Commission invoked its new rule of law conditionality mechanism. Utilizing a model of cross-sectoral bargaining, this article examines how the standoff over democratic standards has bled into the realm of international security. We argue that Hungary has increasingly utilized "hostage-taking" strategies, linking domestic disputes to critical foreign policy decisions—most notably the EU’s multi-billion euro aid packages for Ukraine and NATO enlargement. While the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) was traditionally viewed as a professionalized, insulated domain of consensus, our findings reveal a deliberate instrumentalization of foreign policy to extract concessions in intra-EU politics. This shift reflects a broader trend toward the populist "domestication" of foreign policy, where international stability is traded for domestic political leverage.
